perturbation.original_text: Big Sky and Holdings agree that, at the conclusion of their review of the Information, or within three business days of the other's request, all copies of the Information, in any form whatsoever, will be delivered by Holdings and its Representatives to Big Sky.
perturbation.changed_text: Big Sky and Holdings agree that, at the conclusion of their review of the Information, or within thirty business days of the other's request, all copies of the Information, in any form whatsoever, will be delivered by Holdings and its Representatives to Big Sky.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return deadline of confidential information from three business days to thirty business days potentially violates Minnesota Statutes regarding data privacy and trade secret protection, as it allows an unreasonably long period for potential misuse or accidental disclosure of sensitive data.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.03 states that a court may grant an injunction against misappropriation of a trade secret. By extending the timeframe for returning confidential information to thirty business days, the agreement increases the risk of misappropriation, potentially conflicting with the intent of the statute to protect trade secrets through timely action. A delay of thirty days could be deemed unreasonable and thus weaken the trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.03 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.03 325C.02 325C.04 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.03 Version List 325C.03 DAMAGES. (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under paragraph (a). History: 1980 c 594 s 5 ; 1987 c 1 s 2 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the timeframe for returning confidential information from three to thirty business days. This change creates a potential contradiction with Minnesota Statutes § 325C.03, which addresses misappropriation of trade secrets. While the statute itself does not specify a return deadline for confidential information, the Law Explanation argues that a longer return period increases the risk of misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which includes section 325C.03, discusses damages for misappropriation, indicating the legal importance of preventing unauthorized disclosure or use of trade secrets. The extended timeframe weakens the protection afforded by trade secret law, as it allows for a more prolonged period during which misappropriation could occur before the information is returned, potentially conflicting with the intent of the statute to protect trade secrets through timely action.

perturbation.original_text: During such time as the parties are involved in discussions or negotiations concerning the Transaction and for a period of at least 60 days after conclusion thereof, neither party shall engage in any transactions whatsoever regarding the common stock of the other, whether publicly traded or privately held, registered or nonregistered, including, but not limited to, buying, selling, taking or granting options in or making or receiving offers or tender offers to buy, sell, trade, or exchange any stock of the other, without the express written consent of the other and without first fully complying with all applicable securities laws and regulations and the rules of all exchanges upon which the stock of each respective party is traded, provided however, that the foregoing shall not be construed to limit the right of either party to invest in mutual funds or other commercially available investment vehicles that may acquire the stock of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: During such time as the parties are involved in discussions or negotiations concerning the Transaction and for a period of at least 10 days after conclusion thereof, neither party shall engage in any transactions whatsoever regarding the common stock of the other, whether publicly traded or privately held, registered or nonregistered, including, but not limited to, buying, selling, taking or granting options in or making or receiving offers or tender offers to buy, sell, trade, or exchange any stock of the other, without the express written consent of the other and without first fully complying with all applicable securities laws and regulations and the rules of all exchanges upon which the stock of each respective party is traded, provided however, that the foregoing shall not be construed to limit the right of either party to invest in mutual funds or other commercially available investment vehicles that may acquire the stock of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period during which parties are restricted from trading the other's stock from 60 days to 10 days after the conclusion of negotiations could violate SEC Rule 14e-3, which addresses insider trading in the context of tender offers. The shortened period may not be sufficient to prevent trading on non-public information.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 14e-3 prohibits trading on material, nonpublic information relating to a tender offer once substantial steps have been taken to commence a tender offer. By reducing the restricted trading period to only 10 days after the conclusion of negotiations, the agreement increases the risk that parties may trade on non-public information acquired during the negotiation process, potentially violating the rule if the information relates to a planned tender offer.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.14e-3 - Transactions in securities on the basis of material, nonpublic information in the context of tender offers. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.14e-3 - Transactions in securities on the basis of material, nonpublic information in the context of tender offers. CFR prev | next § 240.14e-3 Transactions in securities on the basis of material , nonpublic information in the context of tender offers. (a) If any person has taken a substantial step or steps to commence, or has commenced, a tender offer (the “offering person”), it shall constitute a fraudulent, deceptive or manipulative act or practice within the meaning of section 14(e) of the Act for any other person who is in possession of material information relating to such tender offer which information he knows or has reason to know is nonpublic and which he knows or has reason to know has been acquired directly or indirectly from: (1) The offering person, (2) The issuer of the securities sought or to be sought by such tender offer, or (3) Any officer, director , partner or employee or any other person acting on behalf of the offering person or such issuer , to purchase or sell or cause to be purchased or sold any of such securities or any securities convertible into or exchangeable for any such securities or any option or right to obtain or to dispose of any of the foregoing securities, unless within a reasonable time prior to any purchase or sale such information and its source are publicly disclosed by press release or otherwise. (b) A person other than a natural person shall not violate paragraph (a) of this section if such person shows that: (1) The individual(s) making the investment decision on behalf of such person to purchase or sell any security described in paragraph (a) of this section or to cause any such security to be purchased or sold by or on behalf of others did no
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the restricted trading period from 60 days to 10 days after negotiations conclude. This creates a contradiction with SEC Rule 14e-3, which addresses insider trading in the context of tender offers. The shortened period significantly increases the risk of trading on non-public information, as the original 60-day period likely aimed to provide a buffer against such activity. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that it is a "fraudulent, deceptive or manipulative act or practice within the meaning of section 14(e) of the Act for any other person who is in possession of material information relating to such tender offer which information he knows or has reason to know is nonpublic...to purchase or sell...any of such securities..." By reducing the restricted period, the agreement increases the likelihood that parties will trade on such information, violating the rule.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota applicable to contracts between residents of Minnesota that are to be wholly performed within such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware applicable to contracts between residents of Delaware that are to be wholly performed within such state.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Minnesota to Delaware could potentially conflict with Minnesota Statutes regarding choice of law if the contract has a substantial connection to Minnesota and the Delaware law is contrary to a fundamental policy of Minnesota law. Minnesota Statute 541.05 sets statute of limitations on contracts, and a switch to DE law could shorten or lengthen this period.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 541.05 specifies the statute of limitations for various causes of action, including contract claims. By changing the governing law to Delaware, the agreement could inadvertently shorten the statute of limitations period for contract claims, thus depriving a party of the full time period allowed under Minnesota law to bring a claim. The change could contradict MN law if the agreement is executed in Minnesota, and if Delaware law shortens that statute of limitations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 541.05 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL PROCEDURE Chapter 541 Section 541.05 541.04 541.051 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 541 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 541.05 Version List Recent History 2015 Subd. 1 Amended 2015 c 5 art 15 s 18 2000 Subd. 1 Amended 2000 c 471 s 1 541.05 VARIOUS CASES, SIX YEARS. § Subdivision 1. Six-year limitation. Except where the Uniform Commercial Code otherwise prescribes, the following actions shall be commenced within six years: (1) upon a contract or other obligation, express or implied, as to which no other limitation is expressly prescribed; (2) upon a liability created by statute, other than those arising upon a penalty or forfeiture or where a shorter period is provided by section 541.07 ; (3) for a trespass upon real estate; (4) for taking, detaining, or injuring personal property, including actions for the specific recovery thereof; (5) for criminal conversation, or for any other injury to the person or rights of another, not arising on contract, and not hereinafter enumerated; (6) for relief on the ground of fraud, in which case the cause of action shall not be deemed to have accrued until the discovery by the aggrieved party of the facts constituting the fraud; (7) against sureties upon the official bond of any public officer, whether of the state or of any county, town, school district, or a municipality therein; in which case the limitation shall not begin to run until the term of such officer for which the bond was given shall have expired; (8) for damages caused by a dam, used for commercial purposes; or (9) for assault, battery, false impriso
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Minnesota to Delaware directly impacts the statute of limitations for contract claims, as detailed in Minnesota Statutes § 541.05. Scraped Law Snippet 1 clearly states a six-year limitation for contract actions in Minnesota, unless the UCC prescribes otherwise. By switching to Delaware law, the applicable statute of limitations could be altered, potentially shortening or lengthening the time period allowed to bring a claim, which creates an inconsistency and contradicts the established legal framework within Minnesota. This inconsistency directly falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: In witness whereof, this Agreement has been executed as of the date first written above.
perturbation.changed_text: In witness whereof, this Agreement has been executed as of the date of the closing of the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the execution date to 'the date of the closing of the Transaction' creates ambiguity and uncertainty, potentially conflicting with the Statute of Frauds, which requires contracts to be dated and signed. The Statute of Frauds ensures that contracts are legally enforceable, and this modification hinders that.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute 513.01, the Statute of Frauds, requires certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought. Changing the execution date to the 'date of closing' introduces ambiguity, because the statute requires certainty of obligations. If the agreement is signed at a much earlier date than the actual closing, it might cause enforceability issues by raising doubts about when the obligations became legally binding. This may void the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 513.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes PROPERTY AND PROPERTY INTERESTS Chapter 513 Section 513.01 513.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 513 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 513.01 Version List 513.01 NO ACTION ON AGREEMENT. No action shall be maintained, in either of the following cases, upon any agreement, unless such agreement, or some note or memorandum thereof, expressing the consideration, is in writing, and subscribed by the party charged therewith: (1) every agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof; (2) every special promise to answer for the debt, default or doings of another; (3) every agreement, promise, or undertaking made upon consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; (4) every agreement, promise or undertaking to pay a debt which has been discharged by bankruptcy or insolvency proceedings. History: ( 8456 ) RL s 3483 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the agreement's execution date, potentially conflicting with the Statute of Frauds. The original text specifies execution 'as of the date first written above', while the changed text refers to 'the date of the closing of the Transaction'. This change might create uncertainty about when the obligations became legally binding, which the Statute of Frauds seeks to avoid by requiring agreements to be in writing and signed. According to Minnesota Statute 513.01, 'No action shall be maintained...upon any agreement, unless such agreement, or some note or memorandum thereof, expressing the consideration, is in writing, and subscribed by the party charged therewith'. By introducing ambiguity, the enforceability of the agreement may be compromised, creating a legal contradiction according to the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.
